data.title: Exzeo: Under The Radar And Gleaming
data.description:
I'll be the first one to say that I am not a technology guy. That said, sometimes, as was the case with Nebius Group N.V. (NBIS), the technology part is easy enough to understand, and the value proposition is so obvious, as is the valuation mispricing, that even a value guy like me can lean in. Exzeo Group, Inc. (XZO) is just such a case.

XZO is 82% owned by HCI Group, Inc. (HCI), a Florida-based insurer that deals mostly with property and casualty markets (I'll touch on HCI below). HCI IPO'd 18% of XZO in November at $21. Florida is a treacherous place to write P&C insurance, given the weather. HCI has been unusually successful because it has relied heavily on technology to underwrite its policies. That technology is XZO.

Whereas most insurers judge a home or property's risk by such things as flood plains, which are large areas on a map, XZO allows for specific property risk (e.g., the property might be in a flood plain, but it happens to be on a slight rise that keeps it from flooding). This property-specific analysis is critical. It allows the company to underwrite policies where other insurers don't and/or offer lower premiums than the competition. On the flip side, it helps the company avoid some losses that other insurers with less detailed information incur.

At the time of the IPO, HCI was XZO's only customer, but it had been breaking out numbers as a separate entity for some time, and intracompany pricing is market-based. Q3 revenue was $56 million, and EBITDA was $29 million.

Fourth Quarter Numbers

Q4 was the first full quarter reported as a standalone public company. It was a blowout. Revenue came in at $53.3 million, up from $44.5 million, and EPS was $0.25, up from $0.15. Adjusted EBITDA came in at $28 million, up from $16.8 million, with margins growing to 55% from 46%.

These numbers brought full-year results to $217 million in revenue, EPS to $0.99, and adjusted EBITDA to $111.5 million. With $305 million of cash, no debt, and only $70 million of current contract liabilities, 2025 results leave this company (at $15.88/share) trading at less than 16x earnings and about 10x EBITDA. These are mid-range to low multiples for almost any high-margin business, never mind one that is expanding those margins through double-digit revenue growth.

I believe XZO has been thrown in the bucket of software companies that have been beaten up lately due to perceived AI disintermediation risks. I believe this fear is severely misplaced. Not only does AI allow XZO to automate underwriting, thereby making the tool even more cost-efficient for insurer customers, but it also works on proprietary behind-the-firewall data sets that XZO built over the past 12 years. In essence, XZO has populated its own LLM.

If anything, the company believes AI will be a major net benefit for the company. They commented in the Q4 conference call:

Next, I want to take a few minutes to give our view on the approaching inflection point of AI in the insurance industry and why we think it will be positive for Exzeo. Much of the insurance industry still relies on human underwriters to manually review and touch nearly every policy. In an AI-driven world, that is going to change. Friction will come out of the system. We saw years ago that the industry would eventually be moving towards a fully automated insurance platform, where policies are bound in minutes and administered with little to no human intervention, which is what Exzeo currently does today.

The product's value is proven by the new partners the company has added since going public. One third party was already signed up as a customer by Q3 earnings, and another one signed up during Q4. The company expects these two companies alone to reach $100 million of managed premium this Q1. A potentially much larger customer signing was Tokio Marine Highland. XZO will distribute Tokio Marine's flood insurance in 42 states. This proves the company's technology works outside Florida and gives it the potential to scale its managed premium.

Guidance

Guidance sounds incredibly conservative. The company guided for $25 million of pretax income for Q1 and $120 million for 2025. That's only 9% growth, while managed premium is expected to grow to $1.55 billion, 12% growth. These numbers for Q1 are below Q4 numbers and below the annualized run rate, respectively. Unless momentum changes dramatically from XZO's business with HCI, which seems unlikely given HCI's results, and none of the third-party business catches on, I think the company will exceed these results.

Valuation

Valuation is very compelling in my opinion, given the growth prospects, high EBITDA margins, and high cash conversion. XZO has been caught up in the software maelstrom of the past few weeks. This product is fairly AI-proof in my opinion, as it is a specialized analysis, although AI is a risk in everything these days, I guess.

Market Cap (@$15.88) $1.44 billion
Cash $305 million
Enterprise Value

$1.135 billion
EV/2026 EBITDA (using $123) 9.5x
FCF yield (using $90 million) 8%

I believe that HCI will spin out its stake in XZO sometime in 2027. Until then, it's a small-cap without a huge amount of analyst coverage that might struggle for attention. That said, it's already at a discount to its IPO price (on a market convulsion), and the news stream should be very positive.

If you wanted a discounted way to play XZO, you could just buy HCI. Analysts value the insurance business at $160/share. That implies the XZO stake is worth almost $1.2 billion for free. The main issue is that some people (technology investors) just want to own XZO and don't want any piece of an insurer, so XZO can rocket higher without HCI moving or worse.

Risk

I think the main risks here are whether the company can continue to attract third-party customers and how much of a threat AI is. Hopefully, we see continued third-party signups, and the company can communicate how AI is not a threat to its franchise. Valuation, in my opinion, already discounts quite a bit of skepticism.

Conclusion

This is a little hidden jewel of a business. The next few years can be very exciting.

data.title: AtaiBeckley: BPL-003 Phase 3 Momentum Keeps Me Bullish
data.description:
AtaiBeckley Inc. (ATAI) is a clinical-stage company that develops psychedelic therapies for treatment-resistant depression (TRD) and social anxiety disorder (SAD). It was formed after a combination of Atai Life Sciences and Beckley Psytech. ATAI's pipeline includes BPL-003 for TRD, which will enter Phase 3 in Q2 2026 based on the efficacy shown in its recent Phase 2 trials. They found that this antidepressant's effect was rapid and lasted for weeks. ATAI's pipeline also includes VLS-01 for TRD in Phase 2 and EMP-01 for SAD in Phase 2a. Hence, I believe their R&D progress and healthy runway justify a reiterated “Buy” at these levels for long-term investors willing to speculate on therapeutic psychedelics.

Psychedelic Bet

AtaiBeckley Inc. is a clinical-stage biotech company developing mental health treatments, especially psychedelic-inspired therapies for serious psychiatric conditions. It was founded in 2018 as Atai Life Sciences. The company then changed its name to Atai Beckley N.V in November 2025 after merging with Beckley Psytech. But they later renamed the company again to AtaiBeckley Inc. in December 2025 after it redomiciled from the Netherlands to Delaware. ATAI is now headquartered in New York, NY. I also previously covered ATAI last October, but since then, the stock has declined by about 46.9%, so I thought it was worthwhile updating my thesis on this name.

At its core, I believe that ATAI's thesis is that existing treatments may help with symptoms of depression and anxiety. However, the current standard of care often requires frequent dosing over long periods and doesn't lead to lasting benefits. Fortunately, ATAI's drug candidates aim to solve this issue. They are psychedelic-based compounds that may offer a rapid onset, more durable effects, need less frequent dosing, and promote neuroplasticity.

Additionally, ATAI argues that psychedelic therapies create benefits through two connected layers at the same time. These two key therapeutic components are biological at the brain-level layer, which are coupled with a positive subjective psychological-experience layer. These two layers may reinforce each other and lead to durable symptom improvement. In the brain-level layer, ATAI suggests that its therapies could support brain repair, growth, and even strengthen connections between neurons. In fact, it's possible that ATAI's psychedelics could even change the way neurons communicate between brain regions.

Therefore, if ATAI's bet on psychedelics holds, the potential effect is that the brain becomes more flexible and better integrated. This is key, as it should help solve patients' dysfunctional patterns. And while psychedelics help, ATAI also focuses on the psychological-experience layer. ATAI's belief is that the intense experience of using these psychedelics may be therapeutic by itself. The experience includes an altered state of consciousness, where patients undergo a perceptual shift of themselves and the world. This includes a holistic cognitive reappraisal, where it's possible to reframe painful memories. This way, patients can ultimately reach emotional release, being able to process or express emotions previously suppressed.

ATAI's Clinical Pipeline

With that in mind, ATAI's pipeline currently includes the candidate BPL-003 for treatment-resistant depression (TRD). It's also worth mentioning that BPL-003 obtained FDA Breakthrough Therapy Designation. Technically speaking, this is a mebufotenin benzoate nasal spray, or a formulation of a psychedelic-based 5-MeO-DMT candidate. The company completed BPL-003's End-of-Phase 2 (EOP 2) interaction with the FDA in Q1 2026 and plans to start Phase 3 in Q2 2026. We also should get its topline data readout by early 2029.

Moreover, ATAI recently reported that its Phase 2 trials showed that one dose of BPL-003 in a cohort of 12 patients led to encouraging results. These psychological benefits are measured according to the Montgomery-Åsberg Depression Rating Scale (MADRS). Essentially, the average score started at 27.5, which is consistent with serious depression, and fell to 14.8 by day 2 (equivalent to an improvement of 12.6 points). Notably, the improvement seemed sustainable at least until day 85 of the trial. And the candidate demonstrated a positive safety profile with no major side effects that could derail its R&D progress.

Aside from BPL-003, ATAI's pipeline also has VLS-01 for TRD. This candidate is in Phase 2, with topline Phase 2 data expected by 2H2026. VLS-01 is DMT delivered through buccal film. It can produce very rapid and intense altered states of consciousness, with a shorter duration, which could make it more practical in a clinical setting. Similarly, EMP-01 is another candidate. EMP-01 is in Phase 2a for social anxiety disorder (SAD), and ATAI reported its topline data in February 2026.

The trial showed promising efficacy results, as the difference between the EMP-01 vs. placebo groups was a reduction of 11.85 points on the Liebowitz Social Anxiety Scale (LSAS). This was measured for patients using EMP-01 at day 43. Furthermore, using the Clinician Global Impression-Improvement (CGI-I) scale, 49% of EMP-01 patients were rated as “very much improved” or “much improved,” versus 15% on placebo. So, it's clear that ATAI's pipeline has several interesting candidates that are progressing and heading into late-stage trials in the near term.

Valuation And Risk Analysis

Now, from a valuation perspective, ATAI currently trades at a $1.3 billion market cap. Its latest 10-K shows its balance sheet holds $85.3 million in cash and $135.4 million in short-term securities. This amounts to approximately $220.7 million in liquid funds against no financial debt (aside from regular operating liabilities). As such, I estimate ATAI's EV at roughly $1.1 billion, and its book value reached $222.0 million. This does price ATAI at a relatively expensive 5.9 P/B, which is considerably higher than its peers' median P/B of 2.7.

And unfortunately, ATAI still doesn't have any approved products, and its revenues for FY2025 came only from licensing and R&D services (about $4.1 million). So, at this point, the bull case rests entirely on ATAI's ability to actually obtain approval for any of its pipeline candidates. In particular, I think the main value driver is probably BPL-003, as the recent trial data suggests it could have a best-in-class profile for TRD.

Additionally, TRD's TAM is projected to reach $4.8 billion by 2035, so it's undoubtedly a sizeable market opportunity for BPL-003. And, if ATAI taps into this TAM with a potentially best-in-class IP, it should position the company very well for a healthy recurrent revenue vertical. Plus, this asset is now heading into Phase 3 by 2H2026, with the company reaffirming it has an ample cash runway at least through 2029's topline readouts from both parallel Phase 3 trials on BPL-003.

Having said that, I see two main risks with ATAI at this point. First, this is still undoubtedly a long-term bull case, as BPL-003 is the leading candidate and its Phase 3 data readouts remain years away. Meanwhile, the company will likely keep burning through its cash reserves, and management may issue more stock to help fund its R&D in the process. In fact, I estimate that during 2025, ATAI burned through $103.6 million. Note that I got this figure by simply adding its 2025 cash flows from operations and CAPEX. But this figure suggests ATAI could eat through its liquid reserves in 2.1 years, which is an issue given that my bull case on ATAI will take at least until 2029 to fully play out.

Secondly, it's not guaranteed that ATAI will ultimately succeed with any of its candidates. And, as previously mentioned, its cash reserves can run low in just a couple of years. So, by the time we have a clearer picture of BPL-003's approval prospects, their runway will be considerably tighter. If at that point they don't have favorable trial data, it could leave investors with failed psychedelic IP and little cash to continue, which I imagine would easily translate into a much lower stock price. This is especially true since ATAI's current valuation multiples already seem considerably higher relative to its peers.

Conclusion: Long-Term Bullish

Overall, ATAI's psychedelic pipeline keeps me bullish in the end. What ultimately nudges me into a speculative "Buy" is that BPL-003's data continues to be positive, and this asset is now heading into two parallel Phase 3 trials starting Q2 2026. Moreover, the rest of their pipeline has promising assets that would be indirectly validated by BPL-003 if it reaches FDA approval at some point in the future. Plus, we do know they have enough runway for the next couple of years, so I'm not overly concerned about any major dilutive events in the near term. Thus, as long as you're willing to hold the shares for the long run, I believe ATAI could pay off nicely in the end. Just make sure you manage your position accordingly.

data.title: EOG Resources: I'm Buying American
data.description:
2026 has been an exceptionally unstable year for oil prices, especially within the United States.

We went from expecting there to be an oversupply of oil across the entire globe when the year started, having that compounded when Venezuelan oil was expected to return at least to the U.S. market if not to the global market, to now, with the current warfare going on in Iran and the closing of the Strait of Hormuz, which oil travels through from the Middle East to the rest of the world, we're expecting there to be a lack of supply.

We can see this in the massive swings in oil prices. Oil has always been a commodity that's been exposed to larger amounts of geopolitical risk than other commodities. Rare earth materials are another commodity that is often impacted by global instability and political tensions, but oil is the hallmark commodity for that in many regards. If one country can rapidly impact the price of a commodity around the globe because of a military action against two different countries, it just reveals how volatile investing in those types of commodities can be.

The companies that generate revenue from these commodities have, in recent decades, focused on controlling production costs. This has led to their break-even price for oil being low; the lower, the better in many regards. However, when it comes to political instability around oil, especially on a global scale, you can still find excellent investment opportunities. One way to find excellent opportunities is to focus on regions where there is lower instability. I find that investing in American-based oil and gas producers or Canadian-based oil and gas producers can provide you with an exceptional level of safety, but also strong returns.

Today, I want to discuss a company that I think is an excellent oil and gas producer. It's primed to reward you as a shareholder but also to benefit from the global instability we're seeing.

Let's dive in!

100% Free Cash Flow To Shareholders

EOG Resources, Inc. (EOG) is a well-known oil and natural gas exploration and production company.

They have operations within the Middle East, the Caribbean, and North America. EOG is an extremely low-cost energy provider, which allows it to have strong capital returns from the oil and natural gas production that it has. When oil and natural gas prices were impacted because of the onslaught of Venezuelan oil, EOG was not a company that was concerned with its bottom line because they have the effective ability to withstand moving oil prices. Looking into the Middle East and the recent bombing in Iran, oil prices continue to be extremely volatile, and unlike other commodities, oil is more sensitive to geopolitical events. This can make oil, at times, a riskier investment realm as a whole, but oil companies have been very effective at ensuring that their break-even point is very low. We've seen this as well with Canadian Natural Resources Limited (CNQ), where they have focused on keeping a low break-even point so that they can focus on developing more production and rewarding shareholders handsomely. EOG falls into this same umbrella, primarily enjoying production across the United States that provides them with strong capital flow.

When we look into 2025, EOG was extremely proactive in rewarding shareholders. Not only do they actively plan to continue to grow their dividend strongly, but they are primarily focused as well on opportunistic share buybacks and special dividends.

EOG has an extremely strong balance sheet, which allows it to enjoy an A- S&P credit rating. This investment-grade rating allows them to effectively tap debt markets when needed to fund growth, although the company isn't primarily using debt as its means to grow, but rather excess cash flow.

EOG's current yield of 3.19% and five-year compounded annual dividend growth rate of 21.6% give it a Chowder Number of 24%.

EOG trades at a current price to operating cash flow of 7.2x, which is below its 10-year P/OCF average of 7.6x. With continued buybacks and dividend growth planned and the current oil price volatility, we feel comfortable using the 10-year ratio for forward estimates. This means EOG could rise to approximately $156 a share by year's end, giving us a total return of about 18% and continued strong returns through 2031.

Overall, EOG is not as cheap as it has been in recent months, but it is still an exceptional value and an exceptional investment opportunity in our opinion at this time. We see not only dividend growth potential, but with the use of free cash flow to continue to buy back shares, we see that supporting a higher overall P/OCF and increased share value.

Conclusion

EOG is an excellent option to gain exposure to oil and gas without as many of the global risks that exist if you were to invest outside the United States. Furthermore, it allows you to invest in a more regionally focused oil and gas producer than some major global names that have operations all around the globe. This allows you to maintain a primarily North American focus while still benefiting from the oil pricing instability around the globe. One major benefit, in my opinion, of EOG is its shareholder focus as far as rewarding shareholders as a whole.

When building a fortress portfolio to surround yourself with, you do not want investments that will be completely knocked off kilter because of a news story that may come out. If your investments are primarily focused on momentum or trading around the news, then you're always going to have to babysit your portfolio. If you want to be able to focus on the best parts of your life, which, for most, will not be the market, you need a portfolio that is stable and strong. No military commander would leave a fortress in disarray. They would ensure it is well established and defensible before they depart on any expedition. Likewise, if you want a fortress-quality portfolio that allows you to sleep at night, you need to make sure it is built with quality holdings.

data.title: Petrobras: Compelling Valuation At Current Price Level
data.description:
Introduction

Petrobras (PBR) has gained nearly 68% since my last analysis on the company back in October, as I rated it a hold based on its position in Brazil and not its deep undervalued nature. With what has been happening in the broad world, I want to assess if PBR is actually better suited for your portfolio than what I first believed.

Current Dynamics

I’ll first quickly go through the latest earnings to have a glimpse at how the company has been doing in a then low-price environment. Petrobras’s net income came in at $4.75B, excluding one-off events, resulting in an EPS GAAP of 44 cents. Top-line wise, it reached $23.6B, a beat of more than $970MM. This validates the company’s push into volume growth to hedge the decline in average Brent prices at the time. On an EBITDA front, it reached $43.8B for the full year, which represents a controlled 4.6% decrease compared to 2024, though Brent decreased by 14.5% on average during the year. It underscored the quality of the asset base that continues to have some of the lowest lifting costs in deepwater drilling. Though it is important to note that the 161% Y/Y increase in net profit was mostly thanks to the appreciation of the Brazilian real against the USD.

Looking ahead, Petrobras unveiled its plan for the next five years through 2030, which provides a strong window in the company’s LT capital allocation strategy. It will build twin pillars of profitable E&P expansion and a pragmatic approach to the energy transition. The total investment is set to reach $109B, split between an implementation side and an under evaluation side that is still undergoing technical and economic validation. The plan targets a peak oil production of 2.7MM bpd by 2028 and a total to exceed 3.4MM bpd by 2029.

I also want to touch on the Brazilian Equatorial Margin, which is viewed as the last great frontier for domestic oil exploration with the same characteristics as the prolific discoveries made in Guyana and Suriname. After years of regulatory hurdles, PBR was issued an operating license to drill an exploratory well in block FZA-M-059 in the Foz do Amazonas basin. It is huge, as the Equatorial Margin could produce up to 1.1MM bpd, a volume that would place it on par with major pre-salt fields.

Though right now Petrobras has been surfing on the major oil shock that is the current escalation in the conflict with Iran, which drove Brent upwards of $100 a barrel, creating severe price distortions in Brazil, outside of just higher realized prices for PR. See the gap between local diesel prices and benchmarks reached a record high, thus private distributors have become reluctant to sell fuel at higher international prices without the ability to pass those costs to customers. This threatened Brazil with widespread fuel shortages, which put pressure on Petrobras, which had to increase the price of diesel sold to distributors by R$0.38 per liter in a bid to narrow the gap with the international benchmarks. At the same time, the government intervened to shield consumers from the full impact of rising energy costs, limiting PBR’s ability to benefit from the global oil price spike. By taxing overall exports, the government is essentially impacting short-term refining margins and dividend capacity.

This highlights yet another example of government interventionism, to which I was skeptical the last time I assessed the company’s prospects. The assets are top quality, that’s for sure, but the risk of intervention continues to be a massive question mark.

Valuation

Petrobras’s valuation is clearly at the rock bottom of the market with a P/E GAAP of 5.59, well below American peers like Exxon at 23.7 and European ones such as Equinor at 19.10. PBR’s EV/EBITDA follows the same trend at 4.89 and the company’s FCF generation is so strong that despite the high rise in stock price, the energy firm still has an FCF yield of 14.5%. The dividend yield also remains highly attractive at around 7% despite the huge appreciation. Though the plan of spending $109B in the next five years will certainly be a drag on near-term FCF and dividend upside.

On other growth fronts, PBR is expanding its refining capacity and shifting its product mix towards high demand and low-carbon fuels. The processing capacity is set to reach 2.1MM bpd by 2030 through debottlenecking and modernization of existing plants. That way it will avoid the massive capital requirements and long lead times of new construction while maximizing the efficiency of current assets that have already reached a record of 94% utilization factor. Moreover, the shift will target higher -value products so as to increase the share of diesel S-10 and jet fuel to account for 45% of total production by 2030. Reinvesting in refining and biorefining will provide a floor for LT cash flow and diversify the revenue stream toward the energy transition.

Petrobras’s great profitability is also linked to the fairly low lifting costs in the pre-salt assets that are apparently below $6 a barrel, which is simply unheard of. Moreover, the company has stated that Brent breakeven for net debt neutrality is at $59/bbl and is set to be reduced to $48/bbl by 2030, which, at the current price, PBR will potentially earn enough to already drastically remove a lot of its debt and accelerate the timeline. More so with a multi-year horizon, it is important to grasp the competitiveness of pre-salt assets as they do not require $100 oil to generate massive cash flows.

Risks

The main risk that has always bothered me has been the simple fact that Petrobras is, at the end of the day, a state-controlled company that is subject to the priorities of the Brazilian government and not the common shareholder. That is why the company keeps on having a valuation discount.

This has been highlighted by the intervention to shield domestic consumers from inflation through export tax price subsidies. For example, there will be an imposition of a 12% tax on crude exports and a 50% tax on diesel exports that will cap the company’s ability to tap the international markets.

Conclusion

I might have been too harsh by judging PBR as a hold, considering that it had all of the proponents that I generally like in potential investments. As I see the current conflict escalating and thus increasing prices, I believe that Petrobras is heading higher and its valuation means that it has a longer road to appreciate than its peers. In that regard, I improve my rating to a Buy.

data.title: Oil Markets Face A Supply Shock - And The Offsets Aren't Enough
data.description:
By Christopher Gannatti, CFA and Nitesh Shah

Energy markets have once again been thrust into the spotlight. In recent weeks, geopolitical tensions in the Middle East have pushed Brent crude back above $100 per barrel and triggered sharp moves across oil, gas and refined products markets. The catalyst is simple but profound: a disruption of flows through the Strait of Hormuz, one of the most critical arteries for the global energy trade.

Roughly 20 million barrels of oil per day pass through the Strait of Hormuz, representing a significant share of global supply. When that flow is threatened—even partially—the consequences ripple quickly across markets.

How resilient is the global energy system to such a shock? And what would it take to stabilize prices?

To understand these dynamics, we spoke with Aldo Spanjer, Global Head of Energy Strategy at BNP Paribas, who provided a detailed breakdown of what is happening beneath the surface of the oil market.

The Scale of the Oil Supply Disruption

At first glance, there appear to be several potential offsets to mitigate supply disruptions: strategic petroleum reserves, alternative pipeline routes and sanctioned barrels waiting for buyers.

Yet when placed alongside the scale of lost flows, the available alternatives appear relatively modest.

“There are a few offsets, but they are not going to get you close to the 20 million barrels per day you’re effectively losing,” Aldo explained. “It’s a patch—not a solution.”

The International Energy Agency’s strategic reserve release is the most immediate response. A planned release of around 400 million barrels sounds significant, but the math quickly reveals its limitation.

At current disruption levels, that volume would only cover around 20 days of lost flows.

Even more importantly, strategic reserves can only be released at a limited daily rate. Historically, drawdowns have averaged around 1–1.5 million barrels per day, meaning they can replace only a fraction of disrupted supply.

In short, reserve releases can buy time, but they cannot fully rebalance the market.

Pipelines and Alternative Routes

Another potential workaround lies in pipelines that bypass the Strait of Hormuz.

Saudi Arabia and the United Arab Emirates both operate infrastructure designed to route oil exports away from the strait. The largest of these is the Saudi pipeline running from the Gulf to the Red Sea.

In theory, this pipeline has a capacity of around 7 million barrels per day. However, practical limitations reduce the usable volume.

“Pipeline capacity is one thing—but you also need ports and shipping logistics that can handle that much oil,” Aldo noted.

The Red Sea export terminal that receives these flows can handle roughly 4–4.5 million barrels per day, leaving only a few million barrels of spare capacity at best.

Even that infrastructure may not be entirely secure.

“If someone wants to disrupt the flows, these pipelines themselves can become targets,” Aldo added.

This highlights a key theme of the current crisis: logistics are as important as production. Even if oil exists in the ground, moving it safely to market is another challenge entirely.

Production Shut-Ins and the Risk of Escalation

One of the most striking developments has been the rapid rise in production shut-ins across the region.

As export routes become constrained and storage fills up, producers have been forced to reduce output.

Current estimates suggest around 7 million barrels per day of production has already been shut in, according to Aldo.

Some of these shutdowns are precautionary, allowing production to return quickly if conditions improve. Others could take longer to restart, particularly in older fields where interruptions can damage reservoirs.

“Seven million barrels a day is massive,” Aldo said.

If the disruption persists, that number could rise further. Tanker availability is already tightening, and storage capacity in many producing countries is limited.

This makes the duration of the conflict the key variable for markets.

“The longer the war goes on, the harder the pain becomes,” Aldo explained.

Why Oil Prices Could Keep Rising

Oil prices have already experienced dramatic volatility, swinging between $90 and $120 within a single week in the early stages of the crisis.

While headlines often focus on extreme scenarios—such as oil reaching $200 per barrel—the more likely trajectory depends on how quickly the geopolitical situation stabilizes.

If disruptions continue for months, the oil market could face a much deeper imbalance.

“If you keep it out long enough, prices will just keep ticking higher,” Aldo warned.

The reason is simple: demand destruction becomes the only effective balancing mechanism when supply is constrained.

Historically, removing 20 million barrels per day of demand from the system has only occurred during extreme events such as the COVID-19 lockdowns.

In other words, stabilizing the oil market under such conditions would likely require a global economic slowdown.

Natural Gas: An Even Tighter Market

While oil dominates headlines, the natural gas market may face an even greater vulnerability.

Qatar is one of the world’s largest exporters of liquefied natural gas (LNG). Disruption to shipping routes in the Gulf could remove roughly 20% of global LNG supply.

Unlike oil, LNG markets have far fewer alternatives.

There are no strategic reserves for gas, and LNG export facilities typically operate near maximum capacity. This leaves little room to increase supply quickly.

“Compared to oil, gas is much more at risk,” Aldo said.

Even though most Qatari LNG flows to Asia, the impact would still be felt in Europe.

LNG markets are globally integrated. If Asian buyers suddenly scramble for alternative supply, they would compete with Europe for cargoes—particularly shipments from the United States.

That competition would push prices higher everywhere.

“LNG is global. If Asia starts bidding for cargoes, everyone pays more,” Aldo explained.

For Europe, this matters greatly. The region relies heavily on LNG imports to refill storage ahead of winter.

A prolonged disruption could leave Europe entering winter with significantly lower reserves.

Strategy Implications for Investors

From an investment perspective, energy market disruptions reinforce the role commodities can play in diversified portfolios.

Periods of geopolitical stress often coincide with rising inflation risks, falling correlations between commodities and traditional assets, and heightened volatility across financial markets.

Commodity allocations can act as both inflation hedges and diversification tools in such environments. However, implementation matters.

The WisdomTree Enhanced Commodity Strategy Fund (GCC) is an actively managed exchange-traded fund and intends to provide broad-based exposure to the following four commodity sectors: Energy, Agriculture, Industrial Metals, and Precious Metals primarily through investments in futures contracts. The emphasis of the strategy is on diversification as opposed to having the bulk of exposure in any one of these commodity groups. If the primary outcome of the crisis ends up being volatility and a lowering of inter-commodity correlations, it could be a particularly compelling broad commodity strategy.

Important Risks Related to this Article

There are risks associated with investing including possible loss of principal. An investment in this Fund is speculative, involves a substantial degree of risk, and should not constitute an investor's entire portfolio. data.title: Dupixent's Impact On Sanofi: A Quantitative Projection Of Its Revenue
data.description:
Sanofi (SNY) is an established pharmaceutical company with an historic capitalisation of around $120B. The share price recently experienced a sharp decline of 10% compared to its historical price due to several setbacks in drugs currently in clinical stage . Their main product, Dupixent, is facing a patent cliff with an expiration date starting in 2030. In this review, we propose a projection of the revenue of Dupixent for each of its indications. We next provide a quantitative approach to find the part of Dupixent's revenue in the market capitalisation of the company. Finally, we will conclude that the stock is an Hold depending on risk tolerance of the investor.

About Dupixent

Dupixent (dupilumab) is a fully human monoclonal antibody that inhibits interleukin-4 (IL-4) and interleukin-13 (IL-13) signalling by specifically binding to the IL-4Rα subunit shared by the IL-4 and IL-13 receptor complexes. By blocking these key drivers of type 2 inflammation, Dupixent helps to reduce inflammatory responses in multiple diseases.

The drug is currently approved worldwide for several indications including moderate-to-severe atopic dermatitis, asthma, chronic rhinosinusitis with nasal polyposis (CRSwNP), chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD), eosinophilic esophagitis (EoE), and prurigo nodularis. In the new and incoming approval we can cite bullous pemphigoïd, chronic spontaneous urticaria or allergic fungal rhinosinusitis.

As a general indication, according to third party sources, the current revenue breakdown is as follow: 65% atopic dermatitis, 20% asthma, 10% CRSwNP, 5% COPD, 1% EoE and prurigo.

Atopic Dermatitis

About the market

Atopic dermatitis (AD) is a chronic inflammatory skin disease. Global prevalence was estimated at around 2.6% representing around 200M patients. Among patients, 36.2% were presenting moderate AD and 18.2% had severe AD. This creates a total addressable market of 64.4M of patients. In practice, only patients resistant to topical therapy will be treated with systemic therapy such as Dupixent. Down the road, it is estimated only around 2% of the patients receive biologics, setting a true TAM of 4M of patients. CAGR for this market was reported to be around 9% from 2025 to 2030.

Revenue projection in AD

Following third party researches, AD represents 65% of the total sales of Dupixent. With a total net sales of $15.7B in 2025, this would represent a revenue of $10.21B. As a base thesis, we will assume a constant market share, growing at the CAGR until 2030, which is the start of the patent cliff faced by Dupixent. Here below is the projection of revenue at 9% CAGR.

Year 2026 2027 2028 2029 2030
Annual Revenue (AD only) $11.13B $12.13B $13.22B $14.41B $15.71B

Erosion due to Lebrikizumab

Dupixent is currently the gold standard for moderate-to-severe AD. Other biologics with similar mechanism of actions such as Tralokinumab or JAK inhibitors are available. However, their adoption remained limited as Dupixent exhibits higher survival rates and better effectiveness on symptomatology with a lower discontinuation rates due to inefficacy or adverse events. Thus, we do not expect them to erode the market share of Dupixent until 2030.

In 2024, Eli Lilly presented Lebrikizumab, an antibody targeting IL-13. With similar effectiveness and safety profile, Lebrikizumab presents a more friendly dosage regimen with one injection every 4 weeks for maintenance compared to every 2 weeks for Dupixent. Analysts expect a top revenue of $1.5B to $3B in 2030. This revenue will derive from new patients directly undergoing Lebrikizumab or from capturing a market share from Dupixent. Below is a table showing the sensitivity analysis of the market share captured by Lebrikizumab on Dupixent's revenue. From our analysis, given the relative competitive advantage of Lebrikizumab and the well established position of Dupixent's, we expect Lebrikizumab to capture between 0% and 20% of Dupixent's shares. To note, for each indications, probabilities of each erosion rate will be modeled later.

Market Share Captured by Lebrikizumab /Year 2026 2027 2028 2029 2030
0% $11.13B $12.13B $13.22B $14.41B $15.71B
5% $10.57B $11.52B $12.56B $13.69B $14.92B
10% $10.02B $10.91B $11.90B $12.97B $14.14B
15% $9.46B $10.31B $11.24B $12.25B $13.35B
20% $8.90B $9.70B $10.57B $11.53B $12.57B

Here we assume a peak penetration of Lebrikizumab as soon as in 2026, which remains unlikely for penetration above 10%. However, we can note that in 2030, the difference between the 10% and 20% penetration revenue and the 0% penetration revenue are close to the expected peak revenue of analysts for Lebrikizumab ($1.5B and $3.0B), suggesting similar assumptions.

Asthma

About the market

Asthma is a respiratory disease characterised by an increased contraction of the airways. Patients with asthma often experience exacerbations, a frequent cause of hospital admissions. Dupixent is indicated as a baseline treatment to prevent those exacerbations in moderate to severe asthma. Importantly, only patient with type 2 airway inflammation benefit from Dupixent.

Revenue projection in Asthma

From the company data, around 165'000 asthma patients benefit from Dupixent. At an annual price of $25'000, we obtain a revenue of $4.13B. This is close to the estimated 20% share of total revenue as detailed earlier. Asthma drug market is expected to grow at a CAGR of 5.3% between 2023 and 2030. Again, for baseline assumptions, here is the projected revenue of Dupixent in asthma accounting for a stable market share at the CAGR of the market.

Year 2026 2027 2028 2029 2030
Annual Revenue (Asthma only) $4.35B $4.58B $4.82B $5.08B $5.35B

A crowded market

Biologics have revolutionised the management of severe asthma. Many biologics with various mechanism of action are available: Omalizumab (anti-IgE), Mepolizumab (Anti-IL-5). These mechanism of action dictate the subtypes of asthma treated, segmenting the severe asthma market even more. Around 7% of the 25M asthma patients have severe asthma. Dupixent is well established in the type 2 inflammation subtype. This indication interests around 60% of severe asthma patients. If around 165'000 patients are treated with Dupixent, we can assume a market share of 20% for it. The following table list the approved biologics for type 2 inflammation asthma.

In practice, the fragmented market of severe asthma is composed of equivalent biologics with no clear dominance. Thus, we would assume the market share of Dupixent would remain close to 20% over the years against the already available treatments. However, margin of improvement for new treatments remain high in severe asthma. Many approaches are currently under trials (anti-IL-33) or already approved (Depemokimab) with similar outcomes but better economics and treatment convenience. Thus, we will account for an erosion of 15% of the market share to model this probability. Below the sensitivity analysis showing the revenue of Dupixent in Asthma in case of erosion.

Market Share Erosion/Year 2026 2027 2028 2029 2030
0% $4.35B $4.58B $4.82B $5.08B $5.35B
5% $4.13B $4.35B $4.58B $4.83B $5.08B
10% $3.92B $4.12B $4.34B $4.57B $4.82B
15% $3.70B $3.89B $4.10B $4.32B $4.55B

Chronic Rhinosinusitis with Nasal Polyposis (CRSwNP)

About the market

Chronic rhinosinusitis with nasal polyposis (CRSwNP) is a type 2 inflammation disease, characterised by a chronic inflammation of the nasal and paranasal sinuses. The disease is associated with impairing symptoms such as nasal obstruction and sleep disturbance leading to a diminished quality of life. Around 0.5% of the population worldwide is concerned by CRSwNP. From real-world studies, 1% of those patients are regularly treated with one biologics. Most of patients receive Dupixent (90%). To note that among this cohort, 35% of the patients also presents moderate-to-severe asthma. Since they may also receive Dupixent in treatment of their asthma, we will remove those 35% to obtain the revenue of Dupixent in "CRSwNP only". We obtain a revenue of around $730M for the US + EU + Japan zones. CAGR for the market is expected to be around 6%, obtaining this baseline revenues table:

Year 2026 2027 2028 2029 2030
Annual Revenue (CRSwNP only) $0.73B $0.78B $0.82B $0.87B $0.92B

Impact of Depemokimab

As seen previously, Dupixent is the most prescribed biologic for CRSwNP. This is due to its "first to market" effect and similar results from competitors such as Omalizumab or Mepolizumab. Thus, we do not expect serious threat from already available biologics. However, one drug is entering the field of CRSwNP with similar results to Dupixent: Depemokimab. Competitive advantage resides in its dosage regiment, requiring only 2 treatments per year. Currently approved in EU and Japan, FDA decisions for CRSwNP indication is still under review. Given the well established position of Dupixent but also the strong competitive advantage of Depemokimab, we will compute the erosion of market share for a maximum of 25%.

Market Share Captured by Depemokimab /Year 2026 2027 2028 2029 2030
0% $0.73B $0.78B $0.82B $0.87B $0.92B
5% $0.69B $0.74B $0.78B $0.83B $0.87B
10% $0.66B $0.70B $0.74B $0.78B $0.83B
15% $0.62B $0.66B $0.70B $0.74B $0.78B
20% $0.58B $0.62B $0.66B $0.70B $0.74B
25% $0.55B $0.59B $0.62B $0.65B $0.69B

Upcoming indications

Chronic Obstructive Pulmonary Disease (COPD)

COPD is a chronic disease of the lungs, impacting on the breath of the patient. COPD is widely spread and have a strong impact on quality of life. It is estimated that around 30% of COPD patients present the type 2 subtype which is targeted by Dupixent. It is reported to affect 400M people worldwide with 70% with at least moderate COPD.

In September 2024, Sanofi announced the approval of Dupixent in uncontrolled moderate-to-severe COPD. With an addressable market estimated by the company of 300'000 patients, Dupixent became the first biologic to enter the COPD field.

Analysts expected a top revenue of $2.5B while Sanofi aims for the double. Given the price of Dupixent, this would represent a market penetration of between 30 and 70%. We will now discuss those projections:

First, let's review the results of both the BOREAS and NOTUS trial that led to the approval. Both of the trials indicated around 30% of reduction in the rate of exacerbations in COPD type 2 patients. This reduction appeared in heavily pretreated patients highlighting its effectiveness. In addition, quality of life appeared significantly improved in the BOREAS study, while the effect was more discrete in the NOTUS trial.

Second, time to patent expiration will limit the time in market to only 5 years. This greatly reduce the possible peak of adoption. Heavily pretreated population can be slower to enter given the burden a new treatment represents for the patient. Therefore, we will be more conservative than analysts assigning a peak penetration of 25%.

Considering competition, Mepolizumab recently got approved for the same indication. In its phase 3 trial, Mepolizumab provided a 20% reduction in exacerbations rate with no effect on the quality of life. Thus, despite its more suitable dose regimen, we expect its adoption as second to market to be limited and only slightly reducing Dupixent market share.

Other indications

Here, we have gathered the remaining current or expected future indication for Dupixent. As detailed below, we do not expect the following indications to significantly weight on the total revenue of Dupixent:

Eosinophilic oesophagitis is a disease characterised by a low incidence and only a subset (refractory and after endoscopic intervention) of patients actually receive Dupixent.

Dupixent is under investigation for Lichen Simplex Chronicus. Results of its phase 3 are expected in June 2026. Thus, including reviewing process and market launch, we believe the time remaining will not allow for a significant penetration of the market.

For Prurigo nodularis patients, Dupixent is indicated in moderate-to-severe form. This represents 30% of the population. Among them, only patients resistant to systemic treatments are prescribed Dupixent, making in a small total addressable market compared to other indications.

Regarding Chronic Spontaneous Urticaria, real world numbers indicate than less than 1% of regularly treated patients actually receive a biologic treatment.

Concerning Bullous Pemphigoid, the disease is characterised by a low incidence compared to the other indications.

Finally, Dupixent recently obtained the indication in Allergic Fungal Rhinosinusitis (AFRS). This indication is a subset of chronic rhinosinusitis. Among this subset, only patients relapsing after surgeries are eligible. Revenues from AFRS-only indication will thus be low and/or mixed with CRSwNP revenues.

Financials and Quantitative approaches

In order to refine our revenues projections for each indications, we modelled the erosion due to the loss of market share in each indication. We below provide a bear (maximum expected erosion), neutral (median erosion) and bull (least erosion) for the 2030 revenue. A S-curve was used to model the erosion speed. We integrate the revenue from COPD by modelling a revenue of between $1B and $3B with a probability distribution skewed around $1.9B.

Next, we built a Monte Carlo simulation inputting the assumptions of our revenue tables. Scenarios were generated with CAGR and peak loss % of each indication as variables. COPD revenue was integrated as well (indication D in the graphs). Our results indicated an average revenue for Dupixent of $21.25B in 2030. While management expects a revenue over $22B, we found that this revenue is expected with less than 25% of probability.

To finally discover the impact of Dupixent in the share price and use our assumptions, we used logistic regression to find the expected ratio between Dupixent sales (DS) to market capitalisation (MC) in the coming years. We found the following curve and extrapolated these values from it.

Year 2026 2027 2028 2029 2030
Dupixent annual sales ($B) 16200 17300 18500 19500 21250
Expected DS-to-MC ratio 6.0 5.0 4.0 3.3 2.7
Projected Market Capitalisation ($B) 97200 86500 74000 64350 57375

As we can see, the impact of Dupixent in Sanofi's market capitalisation is dramatically decreasing in the 5 coming years, reflecting its upcoming patent expiration. As soon as in 2026, 20% of the capitalisation (compared to the historical capitalisation of $120B) is driven by other products and innovations. If we compare these value to recent decline due to the previous setback we found that a decline of 10% is already accounted in this part. Thus, we can expect a 10% more fall while the upside could be almost the double to reach the historical valuation back. This upside could happens under following conditions: A surprising revenue from Dupixent, the progression of one of their clinical compound.

Conclusion

Dupixent still have some growth potential and is expected to account for around half of the valuation of Sanofi until its patent expiration. Our projections indicate the 2030 revenue projected by the company will be reached with a 25% probability.

Limitations

The analysis shared here is presenting several limitations: First, we do not account for cross indications from competitors such as Mepolizumab also indicated in asthma. This could impact on the market share of Dupixent. Also our computations account for a 100% penetration of the market. This could not be true and a proper analysis/correction should be applied. Finally, forecasting the revenue of a drug with multiple indications and several competitors in each is highly sensitive. Thus, our projections could not match properly the actual future results.

data.title: Putnam Small Cap Growth Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Key Takeaways

  • Markets: Fourth quarter results were somewhat flat relative to the first three quarters of 2025.
  • Contributors: Stock selection in industrials and financials
  • Detractors: Stock selection in health care and information technology
  • Outlook: We believe that higher-quality companies with pricing power to defend their margins in the new global trade regime may help temper some volatility.

Performance Review

  • For the quarter, the fund underperformed its benchmark, the Russell 2000 Growth Index.
  • Underperformance was driven by stock selection, while sector allocations also weighed on results.
  • Stock selection in health care weighed most on results, followed by holdings in information technology. Offsetting some of this relative underperformance were selections in industrials and financials.
  • Top individual contributors to performance included out-of-benchmark positions in Carpenter Technology (CRS) and Aritizia. Overweight exposure to Enova International was also a strong relative contributor.
  • Top detractors from performance included out-of-benchmark exposure to Doximity (DOCS) and overweight positions in Stride (LRN) and Adtalem Global Education.
  • At the close of the quarter, the largest overweight sectors relative to the benchmark were industrials and consumer discretionary. The fund's largest sector underweights were materials and health care.

Outlook

  • We remain focused on constructing a bottom-up portfolio driven by stock selection.
  • We are generally more cautious in our outlook and we believe that higher-quality companies with pricing power to defend their margins in the new global trade regime may help temper some volatility.
