data.title: 3 REITs To Buy Before Their Dividends Are Hiked
data.description:
I just recently highlighted three popular REITs that are at high risk of cutting their dividend in the coming years.

This left many readers thinking that REITs must be in trouble.

But in reality, it is the opposite. Most REITs are doing just fine, growing their cash flows and dividends. Those that are cutting are the exception, not the rule.

Not only that, but quite a few REITs today present rapid dividend growth potential because their payout ratios are exceptionally low.

I believe that they could enjoy significant upside as they hike their dividends, and this is why I am buying these REITs today ahead of this catalyst.

Vesta REIT (VTMX)

Vesta REIT is the leading industrial REIT in Mexico.

It owns a high-quality portfolio of newly developed properties in prime locations of the country's most attractive manufacturing and distribution hubs, putting it in a great position to benefit from the growing trend of nearshoring.

This nearshoring trend is leading to an explosion in demand for industrial space in Mexico from Western companies.

It is a lot cheaper to produce in Mexico and faster to ship from there, and importantly, it also allows Western companies to reduce their exposure to China in case of a major future Black Swan event, such as an invasion of Taiwan.

As a result, Vesta is enjoying strong demand for its properties.

It has also nicely mitigated risks by enjoying 10+-year-long leases with multinational companies like Walmart (WMT) and Home Depot (HD), primarily in USD; holding low leverage with a 25% LTV, having shareholder-friendly management team; and finally, being now listed on the NYSE.

Even then, the REIT is getting little attention from investors, especially here on Seeking Alpha, and I think that there is one main reason for this.

It is only offering a 2.5% dividend yield, which many investors, especially US-based, will often think is not enough for a REIT operating in an emerging market.

But here is what they are missing. The current payout ratio is only about 36% of its FFO because the REIT is retaining most of it to reinvest in its development pipeline. The REIT is able to build new assets at exceptionally high initial yields in the double digits, creating a lot of value for shareholders.

As a result, they have guided to grow their FFO per share by 10%+ annually through 2030, which would price the REIT at just 10x its 2030 FFO.

Typically, REITs of this quality (assets, balance sheet, management) that grow at this pace commonly trade at closer to 25x FFO. I agree that a discount is warranted for the emerging market exposure, but remember that the REIT has mostly mitigated those risks by structuring long-term leases with multinational companies in USD and being listed in the US.

Even if it repriced at just 20x, that would lead to a doubling of the share price by 2030, and you would also earn dividend income along the way, resulting in very strong returns.

And I think that the REIT could force some of this upside realization by pushing its dividend to a higher level. By 2030, most of its current development projects will be over, and it won't need to retain quite as much of its cash flow, allowing it to bring its payout ratio closer to that of its close peers, which would lead to a doubling of its dividend.

That would serve as a strong catalyst for the stock, but you can now still buy it before other investors realize this.

SBA Communications (SBAC)

There are three main cell tower REITs in the US. Those are American Tower (AMT), Crown Castle (CCI), and SBA Communications.

All three have performed really well over the long run, massively outperforming the broader stock market (SPY).

But the best performer in this sector, by a mile, has been SBAC:

Yet, despite that, it commonly gets the least attention of the three, and that is because it is offering the lowest yield at just 2.7%:

Dividend Yield
SBA Communications 2.7%
American Tower 3.9%
Crown Castle 4.9%

This low dividend yield leads many to think that the REIT must be overvalued.

In reality, it currently sells at one of the lowest multiples in its entire history, despite having a historically strong balance sheet, being larger than ever in scale, and enjoying attractive long-term growth prospects as AI will accelerate the growth of data consumption:

It also sells at the lowest multiple in its peer group, despite having historically traded at a steep premium:

FFO Multiple
SBA Communications 15.5x
American Tower 18.8x
Crown Castle 20x

I think that this is an opportunity, and the management agrees, as they have been buying back shares to take advantage of this discount.

This helps to create value, but in order to really boost the demand for its shares, SBAC could also bump up its dividend over time.

This is exactly what they have been doing, growing the dividend at a double-digit rate in recent years.

I expect this rapid growth to continue and potentially even accelerate, given that their dividend payout ratio still remains at just 30% today.

A major 20-30% hike would surely make a lot of noise and finally earn the company some more attention from dividend investors.

Sun Communities (SUI)

Sun Communities is a residential REIT that you should put on your radar.

Yes, the current dividend yield is fairly low at 3%.

However, that's because the REIT retains nearly half of its cash flow for growth.

Also, it should be noted that the REIT does not own apartment communities or single-family homes. Instead, it focuses on manufactured housing communities and RV parks, which require far less maintenance capex since the tenants typically own their homes/RVs, and the landlord only takes care of the site and associated infrastructure.

Therefore, the payout ratio is even lower than it may seem, as the REIT does not need to retain as much cash flow for maintenance capex.

The REIT recently also sold its marina business to Blackstone (BX), which allowed it to significantly deleverage its balance sheet and buy back a lot of shares, positioning the REIT for stronger and safer growth going forward.

Its organic growth also remains solid, with its same-property NOI growing by about 5% in 2025, showcasing the resilient demand for this supply-constrained property type.

With leverage now having been brought down to a conservative 3.4x, I expect the management to start bumping up its dividend more aggressively, and this could help it unlock more upside.

Case in point, the dividend was just hiked by nearly 8% earlier this month, and I would expect similar or even faster growth to continue, which should give a nice boost to the stock price.

Investor Takeaway

The REIT market will often react negatively to dividend cuts, but very positively to dividend hikes. This makes sense given that most REIT investors are income-oriented.

This can lead to attractive opportunities for active REIT investors to target REITs that are currently overlooked due to low dividend yields but offer rapid dividend growth potential in the coming years.

At High Yield Landlord, we have repetitively added alpha to our portfolio by going after such opportunities.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Revisiting Blue Owl: A Superior Business Nestled In A Complex Ecosystem
data.description:
Introduction

On Nov. 17, 2025, I wrote an article explaining why I believed the business model of Blue Owl Capital Inc. (OWL) and operational track records so far made its stock an attractive long-term investment. My core argument was that Blue Owl operates as an asset-light intermediary in the rapidly growing private credit market, collecting recurring asset management fees for deploying institutional capital into private loans without bearing the same balance-sheet risks that traditional lenders such as banks face.

In that article, I compared the business of Blue Owl to that of a toll collector on the highway of private credit. As long as traffic continued to flow—meaning OWL's asset under management, or AUM, continues to grow, allowing it to have the funds to originate and manage private loans—the toll collector would continue to earn steady management fees. At the time, I considered the stock reasonably valued and believed the company could potentially become a long-term compounder.

Since then, however, my thinking has become more nuanced.

I am not trying to predict a crisis in private credit. Nor am I suggesting that Blue Owl is poorly managed, or concluding that its stock at the current price is a poor investment. On the contrary, the firm’s recent operating results remain impressive. Instead, this article reflects a more modest personal realization: by owning the shares of Blue Owl, I was investing in the very financial ecosystem Blue Owl is nestled in, an ecosystem that I do not truly understand, or at least do not understand well enough to formulate relatively well-informed investment decisions.

That realization ultimately led me to exit my position at a moderate loss.

Strong Operating Performance, Falling Stock Price

To begin with, Blue Owl’s business operations and financial performance have remained strong.

As reported by Seeking Alpha here, the company recently reported that its assets under management exceeded $300 billion, reaching roughly $307 billion by the end of 2025. Fundraising has also remained robust, with more than $17 billion of new capital commitments recorded during the quarter. Management fees—the primary driver of the firm’s revenue—continued to grow. Net management fees increased by roughly 16% year over year, while overall revenues rose about 20% year over year.

In total, the company now generates roughly $2 billion annually in management fees, with operating margins in the mid-40% range. Fee-related earnings have grown rapidly as assets scaled, illustrating the operating leverage inherent in the asset-management model. As Blue Owl has established the lending and distribution infrastructure (i.e., the talent pool and the network in the private credit market), incremental assets under management, or AUM, shall easily translate into even higher profit margins due to the economies of scale that come with increased AUM.

In other words, by conventional measures, the underlying business remains healthy and continues to grow. Yet the stock price has declined significantly, from around $14 to below $9 today, making it one of the most disappointing calls I have made since returning to Seeking Alpha as a contributor last August.

This contrast between strong operating performance and weak stock price suggests that investors are starting to question something more fundamental—whether the broader ecosystem that generates those earnings is sustainable.

As a matter of fact, the stock price has declined so much that in today's highly valued stock market, Blue Owl stock appears to represent a bargain and a classic GARP opportunity (Source: Seeking Alpha).

Year

OWL P/E Ratio (based upon Consensus Except for 2025)

2025 Actual

10.89

2026 Estimated

9.79

2027 Estimated

8.39

2028 Estimated

7.25

And it is not because analysts do not see any growth in its earnings for the foreseeable future, either. To the contrary, the analyst community still forecasts rather healthy and even enviable growth rates of Blue Owl's EPS for the next three years or so (Source: Seeking Alpha).

So, what is the catch? You may naturally ask.

Year

Consensus EPS Estimate Growth Rate

Dec 2026

11.32%

Dec 2027

16.59%

Dec 2028

15.81%

Blue Owl Takes Off Amid the Private Credit Boom

Let's zoom out of Blue Owl's nest and try to see the big picture of the ecosystem it lives in.

Over the past decade, private credit has become one of the fastest-growing segments of global finance. Following the Global Financial Crisis, many traditional banks reduced their participation in certain types of lending due to regulatory changes, including capital requirements. Alternative asset managers stepped in to fill that gap, raising capital from institutional investors such as pension funds, endowments, insurance companies, and wealthy individuals through private credit funds.

The growth of the sector has been remarkable. As reported here, retail-focused private credit funds alone reportedly expanded from approximately $34 billion in AUM in 2021 to more than $220 billion by the end of 2025—a more than six-fold increase in just four years.

For asset managers like Blue Owl, this surge of capital translated into rapidly expanding assets under management and steadily increasing fee income.

To illustrate the economics in simple terms, assume a private credit manager charges 1% of AUM as its annual management fees. As Blue Owl maintains roughly $300 billion of fee-generating AUM, that alone would translate into about $3 billion of recurring annual fee revenue before performance fees. With operating margins around 40–45%, the model can generate very substantial profits as AUM scales up.

This simple math behind Blue Owl’s asset-light model—what I jokingly call my “fourth-grade ”math”—was part of what attracted me to the company in the first place.

Isolated Incidents or Systemic Turbulence?

However, recent developments suggest that the private credit sector may be entering a period of adjustment or even turbulence. The same Seeking Alpha reports indicate that wealthy investors have recently sought to withdraw more than $10 billion from certain private credit funds in the first quarter alone. In response, some funds have restricted withdrawals, highlighting the tension between illiquid underlying assets and investor expectations of periodic liquidity.

In Blue Owl’s case, one of its retail-focused vehicles—Blue Owl Capital Corporation II (OBDC II)—announced (as reported here) that it would no longer offer its regular quarterly redemption mechanism. Instead, investors will receive capital gradually as assets, i.e., loans funded by the cash from their limited partners, are sold or repaid.

This development does not necessarily imply that the underlying loans are defaulting or that the projects funded by the loans are not performing well.

The issue here is more structural than fundamental. Private credit funds typically invest in term loans, so the capital is locked in for much longer periods. The limited partnership interests (assuming the fund is organized as a limited partnership) that the limited partners of a private equity fund hold are not tradable securities like publicly traded bonds or stocks. The tension arises when such limited partners may expect or need a degree of liquidity, especially when the capital market experiences volatility, but the funds decline to redeem their interests.

When sentiment shifts, those limited partners, whether they are wealthy individuals or institutions, may wish or even need to exit from their private credit investments to enhance their liquidity. However, since their funds have been lent out to private borrowers already, they have been reduced into GPUs and servers or bricks and mortar. Consequently, their requests to their fund managers for (premature) redemption of their equity interests in the private credit fund cannot be honored, unless the borrowers have both the obligations and the means to refinance or otherwise prepay the loans, which the borrowers generally do not.

For shareholders of asset managers such as OWL, the risks here are not necessarily loan defaults but something more subtle but potentially equally consequential: redemptions reduce assets under management, and lower AUM translates directly into lower fee income.

Even more concerning than that, when redemption requests are restricted or delayed—even if investors don't have the contractual, let alone legal, rights for redemptions—the situation can create the false perception that either the underlying loans or the asset managers themselves are under stress, or both. Once the confidence in the private credit market in general and/or an asset manager in particular is somewhat eroded, even a false perception can take on a life of its own to trigger a systemic meltdown and/or a downward spiral.

The AI Infrastructure Layer

Blue Owl sits at the crossroad of two powerful trends in today's world: the rapid expansion of private credit and the unprecedented scale of AI-related infrastructure buildout. A substantial portion of its AUM has been deployed to build out digital infrastructure, such as data centers, to support the ongoing AI boom.

Many of these projects involve tenants with strong credit profiles—large technology companies sometimes referred to as “hyper-scalers,” i.e., the Google, Metas, and Microsofts of the world. That fact provides, or should provide, a degree of reassurance or at least risk mitigation, as those technology companies are rather unlikely to run into any serious liquidity issues.

However, the broader question remains uncertain as the global economy is currently funding one of the largest infrastructure build-outs in modern history. Industry estimates suggest that global spending on AI-related data centers and computing infrastructure could reach hundreds of billions of dollars annually over the coming decade.

It is entirely possible that this investment will prove justified. However, it is also possible that the scale of infrastructure being built today exceeds the economic demand that ultimately emerges, or fails to deliver the promised economic returns.

History offers several examples—from railroads to fiber-optic networks—where transformative technologies led to periods of overbuilding before the market eventually stabilized. If AI infrastructure were to follow a similar pattern, the consequences of any severe downturn would hit private credit asset managers that are heavily involved in the financing of these projects, such as Blue Owl, particularly hard. In such a scenario, the traffic on the private credit “highway” might slow to a halt, and the toll collectors would not perform well.

But whenever financial markets, fueled by unprecedented technological advances, i.e., AI, expand so rapidly, to what extent is the growth driven by sustainable microeconomics of the players, or for that matter, the macroeconomics of the globe, and to what extent is it driven by investor enthusiasm?

I don’t think that I have a more intelligent answer to this question than most market participants.

Further, as I pointed out in my first article, Blue Owl’s balance sheet appears manageable, but it could be strengthened to better withstand a downturn. The company is effectively borrowing about $2.8 billion to pay dividends and repurchase shares. Despite the fact that the lion's share of such borrowings are currently rated as investment-grade, this capital allocation decision in light of the $200-plus million interest expenses that resulted therefrom gave me some pause. This risk factor helped me make my decision to sell my shares.

A Contrarian Investor’s Dilemma

Readers who have followed my articles know that I have a contrarian tendency. When I analyze businesses whose economics I understand—whether insurers, homebuilders, or early commercial stage life science companies—I often perform what I call “fourth-grade math.” If that math suggests with relative confidence that a company’s intrinsic value exceeds the market price of its stock, I am usually inclined to double down and buy more when the stock price declines.

In fact, I am constantly puzzled by the tendency of analysts who generally downgrade stocks after their prices fall in response to short-term setbacks rather than upgrading them when valuations become more attractive.

In the case of Blue Owl, however, my usual contrarian instinct did not apply. The issue was not whether the stock had fallen below its intrinsic value. The issue was that I increasingly realized that I was struggling to value a Blue Owl nestled in an exceedingly complex and opaque financial ecosystem at the epic center of a technological revolution.

Evaluating the risks in the private credit market overall would require insight into the long-term economics of AI infrastructure projects as well as the entire ecosystem, and specifically, the foresight as to whether the entire AI boom has led to a capital market bubble (even if AI itself will likely significantly transform how we live and work). This represents an enormous intellectual challenge even for the most accomplished professional investors.

On top of that, it requires the investors to have a balanced and objective view of the current conditions of the entire capital market and macroeconomic trends in general and the foreseeable trends of the private credit market in particular.

I readily admit that those tasks lie outside my circle of competence. This realization made me decide to sell my position in Blue Owl stock at a modest loss.

My Lesson from Centene

The Blue Owl experience also reminded me of another disappointing call (see here) that I made not long ago on Centene Corporation (CNC).

In early August 2025, Centene’s share price declined dramatically and appeared, at least on the surface, to offer an attractive contrarian opportunity. Many investors argued that the company remained profitable, had a large operational base, and was unlikely to stay down for long, let alone disappear, despite its huge regulatory setback.

Those arguments were not unreasonable.

But I wrote my article to caution individual investors to avoid the stock.

That call did not age well. The stock had already fallen significantly, and avoiding it at that moment may have caused some readers to miss its rebound from roughly $25 to $35, coincidentally about the same percentage (35% or so) move as Blue Owl since my buy rating—though in the opposite direction.

I made that call not because I had concluded that the market price exceeded intrinsic value, but because I believed that the underlying risks were too complex for most individual investors, at least myself, to evaluate with reasonable confidence.

At the time, I wrote in the comment section of my article:

“The real question is whether the risks justify the potential returns—and for each individual investor, how well he or she understands those risks and knows how to adjust positions accordingly.”

I further explained that the risks appeared too complex for me to assess intelligently, there were other opportunities that I could analyze with greater confidence, and my research process ultimately convinced me that the stock lay outside my circle of competence.

In short, I should have applied my lesson from Centene to Blue Owl, which is to say, I should not invest or, for that matter, even write stock commentaries, outside my circle of core competence.

Final Thoughts

Blue Owl remains a well-run asset management firm operating in one of the most dynamic segments of modern finance. Investors who understand the private credit ecosystem and the relevant AI sector risk factors in depth may very well conclude that the current market concerns are exaggerated, and the stock is overly penalized for the market jitteriness and underpriced relative to its intrinsic value. For them, the stock may still represent an attractive opportunity. If I have to make a bet, I will actually bet for that.

However, investing is not guesswork or gambling. It requires much more prudence to first preserve and then grow our own limited capital or the capital entrusted to us to manage by other investors.

As an investor, my conclusion here is more modest: the intersection of private credit lending and an unprecedented AI infrastructure buildout has led to a private credit ecosystem whose scale and complexities far exceed my circle of competence. Consequently, the most rational and responsible course of action for me is to step aside, watch, and learn as much as I can from the interesting development here, instead of continuing to hold OWL stock.

In short, this ostensible downgrade by me of the stock to HOLD is more of an admission of my own limitation than any intelligent assessment of the business fundamentals or prospects of Blue Owl Capital Inc., or the private credit market, or the AI revolution.

data.title: Carlisle Companies: This Boring Business Might Be Your Next Big Winner
data.description:
Investment Thesis

I've singled out Carlisle Companies Incorporated (CSL) for this article, as I started picking up shares during 2025 and have continued to add in 2026 with an average price per share of $327.9. At today's price, I continue to consider it an interesting opportunity for long-term investors. As I haven't quite yet built a full position, I expect to continue adding over the coming year.

What makes this company very interesting is its consistently high ROIC, roadmap towards $40 EPS by 2030, and a current slight rebate in terms of valuation. I expect this asset to continue appreciating from the current level while making it through the storms to come before the end of the decade.

The current management team has shown its ability to deliver, achieving the Vision 2025 strategy ahead of time and presenting a strategy extending towards 2030. Financial metrics have stagnated, and the 2026 outlook isn't very appetizing, but as Carlisle has shown, its pivot to being a more focused company is paying off. I believe that management can continue growing the company while continuing to harvest outsized margins. The main risk is associated with the fact that management builds its forward-looking strategic expectations on an anticipated re-roofing boom as a result of a peak within new builds during the early years of the decade. Should a recession hit between now and the end of this decade, which I, personally, expect, it could decrease the likelihood of management successfully achieving its own $40 EPS target by 2030. Instead, the company could land somewhere between the current EPS of $19.4 and the stated target, which would impact the valuation, which appears quite interesting if they do in fact achieve their target.

Company Operations In Brief

As I haven't covered this company before, here follows a brief intro to what it does. Carlisle is a manufacturer and installer of building envelope products and energy-efficient solutions. The word envelope refers to being focused on the physical barrier that separates the inside of the building from the outside world. This means company operations are centred around roofs, walls, and foundations with the purpose of securing a tight building envelope. A high-performance envelope matters for building climate control as well as structural integrity. Imagine a non-tight building envelope, which would result in increased costs for operating the building in connection to keeping it warm, cold, and dry during the year and seasons. Similarly, once water gets past the envelope, it leads to mold, rust, and wood rot.

During the past decade, the company has undergone a strategic transformation, divesting its non-core legacy assets to become a pure-play building products-focused enterprise.

The company traces its origin back to 1917, when it was known as the Carlisle Tire and Rubber Company. For much of the 20th century, it was a diversified industrial conglomerate catering to automotive, foodservice equipment, and aerospace technologies. The modern era of the company, however, is defined by its transition towards construction materials. Today, the company operates through two segments focused on the building envelope. Carlisle Construction Materials focuses primarily on commercial roofing but also on residential. The other segment is Carlisle Weatherproofing Technologies, which provides integrated solutions keeping buildings air- and watertight. Before pivoting towards the current dual focus, the company churned out ROIC levels in the high teens, but today, it's a 22-25% ROIC company with a stated goal of being consistently at the 25% level. This is one of the things I really like about this company and what I always look for, as I believe a management team's ability to deploy capital successfully is key to driving long-term returns.

Carlisle's primary market is North America, which accounts for the vast majority of revenue (>90%). What's attractive about the business model is that Carlisle is not solely dependent on new construction but primarily focuses on the reroofing (replacement) market for commercial properties. It provides resilience to the business, as it supports the recurrent maintenance needs of aging buildings, of which there are a lot, as more than half of all non-residential buildings in the US are 25 years or older, creating a massive market for reroofing.

The current company strategy, Vision 2030, outlines the path for continued company growth, but more on that later.

Recent Financial Performance

Management announced Q4-2025 results on February 3rd, 2026, and closed the book on FY2025.

  • Q4-2025 revenue of $1.128 billion, 0.4% (-3% organic) growth YoY.
  • Adjusted EBITDA margin of 22.1%, a 300 basis point decline YoY.
  • Adjusted EPS of $3.9, a 13% decline YoY

Q4-2025 was impacted by M&A contributions (hence negative organic growth despite top-line growth) and lower volumes from headwinds within “new constructions,” while commercial re-roofing showed solid performance.

  • FY25 revenue came in at $5.02 billion compared to FY24 revenue of $5.0 billion, a 0.3% increase.
  • FY25 free cash flow margin of 19.4% compared to FY24 margin of 18.8%.
  • FY25 operating margin declined to 20% from 22.8% the year before.
  • FY25 adjusted EPS of $19.4 compared to FY24 adjusted EPS of $20.2.
  • FY2026 revenue guidance expected at a low-single digit increase with a modest 50 basis point EBITDA margin improvement; remember it was down 300 basis points in Q4-25.

Essentially, the business development was flat, with management succeeding in squeezing a bit of additional free cash flow out of the operations. The company has $1.1 billion in cash and equivalents on the balance sheet, and with a free cash flow above $1 billion for the full year, the total long-term debt of $2.8 billion shouldn't raise many eyebrows at this point. In fact, the reason behind the existence of debt comes from the fact that management is active within the M&A space, as it operates in a very fragmented industry where opportunities present themselves from time to time.

Guidance for the coming year isn't that exciting either, with a revenue growth outlook that might just keep up with inflation, if at all.

Vision 2030 Strategy

Those who read my articles will know I've covered Texas Instruments Incorporated (TXN) quite a few times, as it's one of my largest investments, a company I've held shares in since 2019. One of the reasons I like Texas Instruments is the management team's very clear commitment to returning excess cash to shareholders via buybacks. The management team of Carlisle is exhibiting the same trend in a similar high ROIC business. This is great for long-term compounding and shows the excellence of the management team's ability in deploying cash.

As is evident from the section just before, the company isn't churning out strong financials at the moment; instead, it's stagnating with a flat outlook. However, that doesn't shake my trust in the management team for several reasons. Since pivoting towards its current focus, management has delivered ahead of intent to elevate its EPS (2025 vision goal of $15 EPS achieved in 2022), while the achievements below speak for themselves.

Management builds the 2030 vision on a foundation consisting of investing in R&D and targeted M&A to drive innovation across the full suite of products and services that will enable the company to continue charging a premium for the Carlisle experience. Just to take an example, management expects to triple R&D as a percentage of revenue from below 1% to 3% annually on the route to 2030, which should increase new products as a share of total revenue from 15% to 25% by 2030.

With a total addressable building envelope market of roughly $70 billion, as estimated by the company itself, Carlisle holds roughly 5% of that share and therefore sees plenty of opportunity for growth in what can best be described as a fragmented market.

Valuation

There are two ways to view this stock. One way is to conclude that the company's growth trajectory is broken with reference to stagnating revenue and declining margins for FY25, as well as the guidance for FY26. Another way, which requires a leap of faith, is to conclude the company is taking a bit of a breather before it accelerates again towards its 2030 vision of achieving EPS of $40.

If the first observations turn out to be true, you'll be paying forward a P/E of 16.5 for a business that's standing still. While this is 8% below its 5-year average P/E of 18, it's unappetizing if this is the forward-looking picture.

If the second observation turns out to be true, you'll be paying a forward P/E of 16.5 for a company that's en route to double its EPS by 2030. If that turns out to be true, and the company should still command a P/E of 16.5, the stock should carry a price tag of $660 per share. If the company should be valued at its 5-year average, the stock should instead be trading at $720 per share by the time we reach 2030. In essence, that's a 110% stock price appreciation between now and 2030, which is four years away, suggesting strong annual returns.

Naturally, whether the company should be valued in line with its long-term average at that point in time will partly be determined by the long-term outlook at that point. Management had deployed roughly $1 billion towards share buybacks annually over the past three years, and while M&A activity could impact the ability to continue doing so, even buying back the equivalent of $2 billion over the coming four years (equal to reducing annual buybacks by 50% compared to the past three years) would reduce the float by 14% at today's share price, which would boost EPS and share price if valuation should remain at current levels regarding P/E. This is a nice cushion in terms of supporting the stock on its journey to finding whatever level the market concludes as fair value, looking four years into the future.

Risks

I want to highlight one risk in particular, which is connected to what management should take place between now and the end of this decade. That is, the peak in new builds two decades ago should result in a lot of need for re-roofing.

There is solid historical evidence as to why this can be forecasted with accuracy; however, if a recession strikes between now and the end of this decade, which I consider likely. It will be a wrench in the machinery for Carlisle, with all variables regarding guiding for growth having to be revisited. That doesn't change the fact that roofs need to be replaced, but investments in such aren't trivial and could be postponed by some, if not many. It could increase building maintenance requirements and costs, and it doesn't change the fact that all businesses require cash to maintain their assets. That could, of course, end up taking the top off the EPS outlook served by management or push the timeline for achieving the aforementioned $40 EPS by 2030.

Conclusion

If you are on the lookout for an industrial stock, specifically within building products, I think Carlisle is an interesting choice. A focused strategy that has taken its ROIC to new levels, strong free cash flows, and a proven track record by the management team in terms of driving consistent growth both top and bottom line while achieving its targets ahead of time. A stated ambition of reaching an EPS of $40, which, given long-term valuation metrics, would suggest strong upside towards the end of this decade. I'm long on the stock and consider this a long-term keeper. The current entry doesn't scream Buy, as the valuation is only with a slight rebate to the long-term average; however, it's a fine entry point for building a position over the coming quarters, I believe.
